Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, and the DPDP Rules, 2025, the processing of a child’s data is subject to strict protective mandates, centered on the requirement of verifiable parental consent. A "child" is defined by the Act as an individual who has not yet completed eighteen years of age.
1. The Statutory Mandate for Verifiable Consent
According to Section 9(1), a Data Fiduciary is legally required to obtain the verifiable consent of a parent or a lawful guardian before processing any personal data belonging to a child. This requirement is part of a broader set of "additional obligations" intended to safeguard minors in the digital ecosystem.
2. The Verification Process (Rule 10)
The DPDP Rules establish a rigorous standard for how this consent must be obtained. A Data Fiduciary must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure the consent is valid and must observe due diligence to verify that the person identifying themselves as the parent is indeed an identifiable adult (someone 18 or older).
3. Methods of Verification
The framework provides several pathways for a fiduciary to verify an adult's identity and age in connection with parental consent:
* Existing Records: Referencing reliable identity and age details already available to the Data Fiduciary (for example, if the parent is already a registered user on the platform).
* Voluntary Disclosure: Using identity and age details voluntarily provided by the individual at the time of the request.
* Virtual Tokens: Utilizing a virtual token issued by an authorized entity that is mapped to the individual's verified identity and age details.
* Digital Locker Services: Verification can also be performed through details made available and verified by a Digital Locker service provider.
4. Prohibitions Regarding Children's Data
The requirement for verifiable consent exists alongside absolute prohibitions on certain types of processing for minors:
* No Detrimental Effect: Fiduciaries must not process data in any way likely to cause a detrimental effect on a child's well-being.
* No Monitoring or Targeting: Fiduciaries are strictly barred from undertaking tracking or behavioural monitoring of children or directing targeted advertising at them.
5. Exemptions and Specialized Processing
Under Section 9(4) and Rule 12, certain classes of Data Fiduciaries and specific purposes are exempt from the requirements of verifiable consent and the prohibition on tracking:
* Healthcare: Clinical and mental health establishments and healthcare professionals are exempt when processing is necessary for providing health services to protect the child's health.
* Education and Safety: Educational institutions may track or monitor children for educational activities or to ensure the safety of students.
* Transport and Childcare: Crèches and transport providers (engaged by schools) may track children's real-time location in the interest of their safety.
* State Functions: Processing by the State for providing subsidies or benefits in the interest of a child is also exempt from these specific Section 9 mandates.
6. Enforcement and Penalties
Failure to observe these obligations is treated with high severity. Under the Act’s Schedule, a breach of the additional obligations related to children can result in a monetary penalty of up to two hundred crore rupees. These provisions are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the November 13, 2025 notification, making them legally enforceable in May 2027

children.title: No detrimental processing to well-being
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the prohibition against processing data in a manner that causes a "detrimental effect on the well-being of a child" is a core statutory mandate designed to ensure the safety and healthy development of minors.
1. The Statutory Prohibition (Section 9)
The Act establishes a strict standard for any entity handling the data of a child, which is defined as an individual who has not yet completed eighteen years of age. Specifically, Section 9(2) mandates that:
* A Data Fiduciary shall not undertake any processing of personal data that is likely to cause any detrimental effect on the well-being of a child.
Unlike the requirements for verifiable parental consent or the ban on tracking, which have specific exemptions for healthcare or education, this prohibition on "detrimental processing" is an absolute obligation for fiduciaries.
2. Integration with Protective Frameworks
This obligation exists within a broader "General Obligations" framework specifically for children, which also includes:
* Prohibition on Tracking: Data Fiduciaries are barred from undertaking tracking or behavioral monitoring of children.
* Prohibition on Targeted Ads: Entities are prohibited from directing targeted advertising at children.
* Verifiable Consent: Fiduciaries must obtain verifiable parental consent before processing a child's personal data.
3. Processing to Prevent Detrimental Effects
Interestingly, the DPDP Rules, 2025, identify the prevention of detrimental effects as a specialized purpose that allows for certain procedural flexibility. Under the Fourth Schedule, a Data Fiduciary is exempt from the strictures of verifiable consent and the tracking ban if the processing is done specifically for:
* Ensuring that any information, service, or advertisement likely to cause a detrimental effect on the well-being of a child is not accessible to her.
This means that while fiduciaries cannot harm a child's well-being, they are encouraged (and procedurally supported) to use data to filter out harmful content or services.
4. Enforcement and High Penalties
The framework treats the protection of children’s well-being with significant gravity. Under the Schedule of the Act, a breach in observing the additional obligations in relation to children under Section 9 can result in a monetary penalty of up to two hundred crore rupees. This is among the highest potential penalties specified in the Act, surpassed only by the penalty for failing to take reasonable security safeguards to prevent a data breach.
5. Implementation Timeline
The substantive provisions governing the processing of children's data (Section 9) and the corresponding procedural mandates (Rules 10 and 12) are subject to a staggered commencement. According to the November 13, 2025, notification, these mandates are scheduled to come into force eighteen months later, meaning the prohibition on detrimental processing will be formally enforceable in May 2027

children.title: No tracking or behavioral monitoring
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, and the DPDP Rules, 2025, the prohibition against tracking or behavioural monitoring of children is a central protective pillar designed to prevent the exploitation of minors' digital footprints,. A "child" is defined under the Act as an individual who has not yet completed the age of eighteen years.
1. The Statutory Prohibition (Section 9)
The Act imposes a strict ban on specific processing activities related to children's data:
* No Tracking or Monitoring: A Data Fiduciary is expressly prohibited from undertaking the tracking or behavioural monitoring of children.
* No Targeted Advertising: Linked to the tracking ban, entities are also barred from directing targeted advertising at children.
2. Exemptions by Class of Data Fiduciary (Fourth Schedule, Part A)
The DPDP Rules, 2025, recognize that in certain specialized contexts, tracking or monitoring is necessary for a child's education or physical safety,. The following entities are exempt from the tracking ban under specific conditions:
* Educational Institutions: Tracking and monitoring are permitted when restricted to educational activities or when done in the interest of student safety,.
* Crèches and Daycare Centers: Individuals entrusted with the care of infants and children in these centers may undertake monitoring if it is restricted to ensuring the safety of the children in their care,.
* Transport Providers: Entities engaged by schools or crèches for transportation are permitted to track the location of children during travel to and from the institution to ensure their safety,.
3. Exemptions by Purpose (Fourth Schedule, Part B)
The prohibition on tracking also does not apply when the processing serves critical public or safety interests,:
* Legal and State Functions: Tracking is permitted to the extent necessary for the exercise of any power or performance of any function in the interest of a child under any Indian law, or for providing subsidies and benefits,,.
* Real-Time Location: Processing is allowed for determining a child's real-time location when it is done in the interest of their safety and protection,.
* Filtering Harmful Content: Fiduciaries may process data to ensure that information, services, or advertisements likely to have a detrimental effect on a child's well-being are not accessible to them,.
4. Potential for "Verifiably Safe" Exemptions
The Act provides a mechanism for the Central Government to relax these restrictions for specific entities. If the Government is satisfied that a Data Fiduciary processes children's data in a verifiably safe manner, it may notify an age above which that specific fiduciary is exempt from the ban on tracking and behavioral monitoring.
5. Enforcement and Penalties
The framework treats the protection of children as a high-priority obligation:
* Significant Penalties: A breach of these additional obligations in relation to children (Section 9) can result in a monetary penalty of up to two hundred crore rupees,.
* Implementation Timeline: According to the enforcement notification from November 13, 2025, the provisions of Section 9 and the corresponding Rule 12 will come into force eighteen months after the notification,,. This makes the ban on tracking formally enforceable in May 2027.

children.title: No targeted advertising
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, and the DPDP Rules, 2025, the prohibition of targeted advertising directed at children is a mandatory additional obligation for Data Fiduciaries. This restriction is designed to protect minors from commercial exploitation and algorithmic manipulation of their preferences and behaviors.
1. Core Statutory Prohibition
A "child" is defined as an individual who has not yet completed eighteen years of age. Under Section 9(3) of the Act, a Data Fiduciary is expressly forbidden from undertaking two related activities regarding children:
* Tracking or behavioural monitoring.
* Targeted advertising directed at them.
2. Context within Children's Protections
The ban on targeted advertising is part of a broader set of stringent obligations intended to safeguard minors:
* Verifiable Parental Consent: Fiduciaries must obtain verifiable consent from a parent or lawful guardian before processing any personal data of a child.
* No Detrimental Processing: Fiduciaries are prohibited from any processing likely to cause a detrimental effect on a child's well-being.
3. Specialized Exemptions
The framework provides specific instances where these restrictions are relaxed to serve the child's safety or the public interest:
* Filtering Harmful Advertisements: Under the Fourth Schedule (Part B), the prohibition on tracking and targeted advertising does not apply when processing is necessary to ensure that advertisements likely to cause a detrimental effect on a child's well-being are not accessible to them. Essentially, fiduciaries can use data to block harmful ads but not to serve targeted ones.
* Verifiably Safe Processing: The Central Government has the power under Section 9(5) to exempt specific Data Fiduciaries from the ban on targeted advertising if it is satisfied that the entity processes children's data in a "verifiably safe manner". In such cases, the government may notify an age above which the exemption applies.
4. Enforcement and Heavy Penalties
The DPDP Act treats the protection of children as a high-priority obligation with severe financial consequences for non-compliance:
* Penalty Amount: A breach of the additional obligations in relation to children under Section 9 (including targeted advertising) can result in a monetary penalty of up to two hundred crore rupees.
* Board Inquiries: The Data Protection Board of India has the authority to inquire into such breaches upon receiving a complaint from a Data Principal or a reference from the Government.
5. Implementation Timeline
According to the enforcement notification issued on November 13, 2025, the substantive provisions governing children's data protections are subject to a staggered commencement:
* Statutory Mandate: Section 9, which establishes the prohibition, is scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the notification.
* Procedural Rule: Rule 12, which outlines the specific exemptions, follows the same eighteen-month timeline.
* Effective Date: Consequently, the ban on targeted advertising directed at children will be formally and legally enforceable starting in May 2027

children.title: Verifiable lawful guardian consent
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, and the DPDP Rules, 2025, the processing of personal data belonging to persons with disabilities is subject to a specialized protective framework centered on obtaining verifiable lawful guardian consent. This requirement ensures that individuals who may be unable to take legally binding decisions are protected through the oversight of a recognized guardian.
1. Definition of Data Principal and Guardian
The Act expands the definition of a Data Principal to ensure representation for those with disabilities. Where an individual is a person with a disability, the term "Data Principal" includes her lawful guardian acting on her behalf.
2. The Statutory Mandate for Verifiable Consent
According to Section 9(1), a Data Fiduciary is legally required to obtain the verifiable consent of the lawful guardian before processing any personal data of a person with a disability who has such a guardian. This is categorized as an "additional obligation" for fiduciaries, similar to the protections afforded to children.
3. The Verification Process (Rule 11)
The DPDP Rules, 2025, establish the due diligence standards that a Data Fiduciary must follow when obtaining this consent.
* Due Diligence: While obtaining consent from someone identifying as a lawful guardian, the fiduciary must verify that the individual was officially appointed under the law applicable to guardianship.
* Recognized Appointing Authorities: The fiduciary must verify the appointment was made by:
   * A court of law.
   * A designated authority (as defined under the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016).
   * A local level committee (constituted under the National Trust Act, 1999).
4. Scope of "Person with Disability"
The Rules provide a specific definition for a "person with disability" in the context of requiring a guardian's consent:
* Individuals with long-term physical, mental, intellectual, or sensory impairments which, in interaction with various barriers, hinder their full and effective participation in society.
* The definition specifically includes those who, despite being provided with adequate and appropriate support, are unable to take legally binding decisions.
* It also encompasses specific conditions such as autism, cerebral palsy, and mental retardation, or combinations of these conditions.
5. The Right to Nominate and Incapacity
In the broader context of rights, the Act also provides for a Right to Nominate. A Data Principal can nominate another individual to exercise her rights in the event of incapacity, which is defined as the inability to exercise rights due to unsoundness of mind or infirmity of body. This ensures that even if an official guardian is not yet in place, the individual’s data interests can be managed by a trusted nominee.
6. Enforcement and Penalties
The framework treats the protection of persons with disabilities with high priority:
* Significant Penalties: A breach of the additional obligations under Section 9 (which includes the failure to obtain verifiable guardian consent) can result in a monetary penalty of up to two hundred crore rupees.
* Implementation Timeline: According to the notification issued on November 13, 2025, the provisions governing the processing of data for persons with disabilities (Section 9 and Rule 11) are subject to a staggered commencement. These mandates are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the notification, making them legally enforceable in May 2027

children.title: Due diligence on guardianship status
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, and the DPDP Rules, 2025, the processing of personal data belonging to persons with disabilities is governed by a strict requirement for verifiable consent from a lawful guardian. To ensure this protection is not bypassed, the framework imposes a specific duty of due diligence on Data Fiduciaries to verify the legal status of any individual claiming to be such a guardian.
1. The Core Mandate for Due Diligence
According to Rule 11(1) of the DPDP Rules, 2025, a Data Fiduciary must observe due diligence when obtaining verifiable consent from an individual identifying herself as a lawful guardian. This process is not merely a formality; the fiduciary is legally required to verify that the guardian was officially appointed under the law applicable to guardianship.
2. Recognized Appointing Authorities
The due diligence process requires fiduciaries to confirm that the guardian's appointment was made by one of three specific legal entities:
* A Court of Law: Guardianship granted through the formal judicial system.
* A Designated Authority: This refers to authorities designated under Section 15 of the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016, specifically tasked with supporting individuals in exercising their legal capacity.
* A Local Level Committee: Committees constituted under Section 13 of the National Trust Act, 1999, which manage guardianship for specific conditions such as autism or cerebral palsy.
3. Defining the Scope of Protection
The requirement for due diligence is triggered when a person with a disability is unable to take legally binding decisions, even when provided with adequate support. The sources define these individuals as having long-term physical, mental, intellectual, or sensory impairments, including specific conditions like:
* Autism.
* Cerebral palsy.
* Mental retardation.
* Severe multiple disabilities.
In these instances, the lawful guardian is legally recognized as part of the "Data Principal" and must act on the individual's behalf.
4. Verifiable Consent and Additional Obligations
The duty of due diligence is a prerequisite for obtaining verifiable consent under Section 9(1) of the Act. This is classified as an "additional obligation" for Data Fiduciaries, placing it on par with the high-level protections afforded to children.
5. Penalties for Failure in Due Diligence
The Act treats the failure to properly verify guardianship and obtain valid consent with extreme gravity. Under the Schedule of the Act, a breach in observing these additional obligations in relation to persons with disabilities (Section 9) can result in a monetary penalty of up to two hundred crore rupees.
6. Implementation Timeline
The specific provisions regarding due diligence on guardianship (Section 9 and Rule 11) are subject to a staggered commencement schedule.
* Notification Date: November 13, 2025.
* Enforcement Date: These mandates are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the notification, meaning fiduciaries must have their due diligence mechanisms operational by May 2027

children.title: Body corporate with perpetual succession
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) is established as a legal entity with a specific corporate status to ensure its institutional continuity and legal autonomy.
Legal Status and Corporate Identity
The Act explicitly defines the Board's legal nature under Section 18(2):
* Body Corporate: The Board is a body corporate by the name of the Data Protection Board of India.
* Perpetual Succession: It possesses perpetual succession, meaning the Board continues to exist as a single legal entity regardless of changes in its membership (Chairperson or other Members).
* Common Seal: The Board is required to have a common seal, which serves as its official signature for formal documents and legal instruments.
Powers Conferred by Corporate Status
Being a body corporate with perpetual succession grants the Board specific legal powers that allow it to function independently:
* Property Ownership: The Board has the power to acquire, hold, and dispose of property, both movable and immovable.
* Contractual Capacity: It has the legal authority to enter into contracts in its own name.
* Legal Standing: The Board can sue or be sued in its own name, allowing it to engage in legal proceedings independently of the Central Government.
Establishment and Structural Context
* Official Establishment: The Central Government formally established the Board through a notification on November 13, 2025.
* Headquarters: The head office of the Data Protection Board is located in the National Capital Region (NCR) of India.
* Composition: As a body corporate, the Board consists of a Chairperson and a specific number of Members (currently notified as four members) appointed by the Central Government.
* Independence: The Board is mandated to function as an independent body and, as far as practicable, operate as a digital office, meaning its processes are digital by design.
Regulatory Framework
The provisions of the Act governing the establishment and corporate status of the Board (Sections 18 to 26) came into force on November 13, 2025. This corporate structure ensures that the Board remains a permanent fixture of the data protection regime in India, capable of exercising its powers—such as conducting inquiries and imposing penalties—as a distinct legal person

children.title: Independent digital office
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, a "digital office" is defined as an institution that utilizes an online mechanism to conduct its proceedings—ranging from the receipt of an initial complaint or reference to the final disposal of the matter—entirely in a digital mode. This concept is central to the operational identity of the Data Protection Board of India (DPBI), which is mandated to function as an independent body.
1. Structural and Functional Independence
The Board is established as a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal, granting it the legal autonomy to own property, enter contracts, and sue or be sued in its own name. Within this framework, the Act explicitly requires the Board to function as an independent body, ensuring that its adjudicatory and regulatory roles are insulated from undue influence.
2. "Digital by Design" Mandate
The Board's operations are intended to be "digital by design," particularly regarding the following activities:
* Case Management: The receipt of complaints, their subsequent allocation to Members, and the scheduling of hearings must be conducted through digital means.
* Adjudication: The hearing process and the final pronouncement of decisions are required to be digital, utilizing prescribed techno-legal measures.
* Administrative Actions: The Board is authorized to observe procedures for its meetings and the authentication of its orders and directions through digital means.
3. Operational Flexibility and Techno-Legal Measures
The DPDP Rules, 2025, provide more specific guidance on how this digital office should function. Rule 20 states that the Board shall adopt techno-legal measures to conduct proceedings in a manner that does not require the physical presence of any individual. Importantly, this digital-first approach does not diminish the Board’s formal legal powers; it retains the same authority as a civil court to summon and enforce attendance or examine individuals on oath, even if those processes occur remotely.
4. Extension to the Appellate Tribunal
The requirement for an independent digital office extends to the Appellate Tribunal as well. Aggrieved persons may file appeals in a digital form, and the Tribunal is mandated to function as a digital office, employing similar techno-legal measures to conduct its hearings without requiring physical presence.
5. Implementation Timeline
While the Central Government formally established the Data Protection Board of India on November 13, 2025, the full transition to a digital office is subject to a staggered commencement.
* Active Provisions: The sections governing the establishment, composition, and administrative powers of the Board (Sections 18–26) came into force on the date of the notification in November 2025.
* Future Mandate: The substantive procedural requirements for the Board to operate as a digital office (Section 28 and Rule 20) are scheduled to come into force eighteen months later, in May 2027

children.title: Inquiry and penalty powers
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) is granted extensive powers to conduct inquiries and impose significant monetary penalties to ensure compliance. These powers are designed to maintain the integrity of the data protection regime while functioning as an independent, "digital-by-design" adjudicatory body.
1. Scope of Inquiry Powers
The Board is empowered to initiate an inquiry under several specific circumstances:
* Data Breaches: Upon receiving an intimation of a personal data breach from a Data Fiduciary.
* Complaints: On a complaint made by a Data Principal regarding a personal data breach, a breach of a fiduciary's obligations, or a violation of an individual's rights.
* Government References: On a reference made by the Central or State Government.
* Intermediaries: On a reference from the Central Government regarding a breach by an intermediary under the Act.
* Consent Managers: In response to a complaint about a Consent Manager’s obligations or a breach of its registration conditions.
2. Civil Court Powers and Procedure
To effectively discharge its functions, the Board is vested with the same powers as a civil court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, in respect of:
* Summoning and enforcing the attendance of any person and examining them on oath.
* Receiving evidence on affidavit and requiring the discovery and production of documents.
* Inspecting data, books, registers, or accounts.
The Board must follow the principles of natural justice and record reasons for its actions. It may also issue interim orders during the course of an inquiry after hearing the concerned parties.
3. Penalty Powers and Determination
If the Board determines, following an inquiry, that a breach of the Act or its Rules is significant, it may impose monetary penalties. In determining the amount, the Board must consider several factors:
* The nature, gravity, and duration of the breach.
* The type and nature of the personal data affected.
* Whether the breach is of a repetitive nature.
* Any gains realized or losses avoided as a result of the breach.
* Actions taken by the person to mitigate the effects of the breach.
4. Specific Penalties (The Schedule)
The Act specifies maximum penalty amounts for different types of violations:
* Failure to take reasonable security safeguards to prevent a data breach: Up to two hundred and fifty crore rupees.
* Failure to notify the Board or individuals of a data breach: Up to two hundred crore rupees.
* Breach of obligations in relation to children: Up to two hundred crore rupees.
* Breach of Significant Data Fiduciary (SDF) obligations: Up to one hundred and fifty crore rupees.
* Breach of Data Principal duties: Up to ten thousand rupees.
* Other breaches of the Act or Rules: May extend to fifty crore rupees.
5. Additional Remedial Actions
Beyond penalties, the Board can:
* Direct urgent remedial or mitigation measures in the event of a breach.
* Issue directions to any person, who is then legally bound to comply.
* Direct parties to attempt resolution through mediation.
* Accept a voluntary undertaking from a person at any stage of the proceeding, which acts as a bar on further proceedings unless the terms are breached.
6. Implementation and Timelines
The Board was formally established on November 13, 2025, and its administrative provisions (Sections 18–26) are currently in force. However, the substantive powers related to inquiries and penalties are subject to a staggered commencement:
* Inquiry/Penalty Provisions: Most sections governing the Board's inquiry and penalty powers (Sections 27, 28, and 33) are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the initial notification, in May 2027.
* Timeline for Completion: Once these powers are active, the Board must complete an inquiry within six months of receiving a complaint or intimation, with a possible one-time extension of three months

children.title: Directional and remedial powers
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) is vested with extensive directional and remedial powers. These powers allow the Board to intervene actively to prevent harm, mitigate the effects of data breaches, and ensure that Data Fiduciaries and Consent Managers adhere to their statutory obligations.
1. Urgent Remedial and Mitigation Measures
The Board has the specific authority to act swiftly in the event of a security failure.
* Breach Response: Upon receiving an intimation of a personal data breach, the Board can direct a Data Fiduciary to undertake urgent remedial or mitigation measures.
* Inquiry and Penalty: These directions often serve as the immediate response while the Board simultaneously conducts a deeper inquiry to determine if a penalty is warranted.
2. General Power to Issue Directions
Beyond breach response, the Board possesses a broad mandate to issue directions to any person to ensure the effective discharge of its functions.
* Binding Nature: Any person to whom a direction is issued is legally bound to comply.
* Procedural Safeguards: Before issuing such directions, the Board must provide the concerned person with an opportunity of being heard and must record its reasons in writing.
* Flexibility: The Board may modify, suspend, withdraw, or cancel its own directions based on a representation from an affected person or a reference from the Central Government.
3. Interim Orders and Voluntary Undertakings
The Act provides the Board with flexible tools to manage ongoing inquiries and encourage self-correction:
* Interim Orders: If necessary during the course of an inquiry, the Board may issue interim orders after recording reasons and hearing the affected party.
* Voluntary Undertakings: The Board may accept a voluntary undertaking from a person at any stage of a proceeding. This undertaking may include a commitment to take specific actions, refrain from others, or publicize the undertaking. Acceptance of such an undertaking typically acts as a bar on further proceedings regarding its contents, unless the terms are breached.
4. Remedial Powers Regarding Consent Managers
The DPDP Rules, 2025, grant the Board specialized remedial powers over Consent Managers:
* Compliance Measures: If a Consent Manager is not adhering to its obligations, the Board can direct it to take specific measures to ensure adherence.
* Registration Actions: In the interest of Data Principals, the Board may suspend or cancel the registration of a Consent Manager.
* Protective Directions: The Board can issue any directions it deems fit to a Consent Manager specifically to protect the interests of Data Principals.
5. Alternate Dispute Resolution (Mediation)
As a remedial alternative to formal inquiries, if the Board believes a complaint can be resolved through mediation, it may direct the parties to attempt resolution through a mutually agreed-upon mediator.
6. Referral for Blocking Orders
In extreme cases of recidivism, the Board's remedial power extends to advising the Central Government.
* Blocking Access: If a Data Fiduciary has had monetary penalties imposed on it in two or more instances, the Board may advise the Government to block public access to the fiduciary's computer resources (such as an app or website) in the interest of the general public.
7. Enforcement Context and Timelines
These directional and remedial powers are exercised by the Board in its capacity as an independent body corporate with the powers of a civil court. While the Board was established in November 2025, the substantive sections granting these powers (Sections 27, 28, and 31–33) and their corresponding rules are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the initial notification, becoming fully operational in May 2027

children.title: Registered with the Board
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, a Consent Manager is defined specifically as a person registered with the Board. This registration is a mandatory prerequisite for any entity wishing to act as a single point of contact for Data Principals to give, manage, review, and withdraw their consent through transparent and interoperable platforms.
1. Core Requirement for Registration
The Act stipulates that every Consent Manager must be registered with the Data Protection Board of India. This registration is subject to various technical, operational, financial, and other conditions prescribed by the government to ensure these entities are fit to handle sensitive consent-related functions.
2. Conditions for Registration (First Schedule, Part A)
To qualify for registration, an applicant must meet several stringent criteria:
* Legal Structure: The applicant must be a company incorporated in India.
* Financial Capacity: The entity must have a net worth of at least two crore rupees and possess a sound financial condition.
* Operational Integrity: The directors and key managerial personnel must have a reputation for fairness and integrity.
* Constitutional Documents: The company’s Memorandum and Articles of Association must include provisions to avoid conflicts of interest with Data Fiduciaries, and these provisions can only be amended with the Board's previous approval.
* Technical Certification: The applicant's interoperable platform must be independently certified to be consistent with data protection standards and assurance frameworks published by the Board.
3. The Registration Process (Rule 4)
The DPDP Rules, 2025, outline the procedural steps for registration:
* Application: A person meeting the conditions must apply to the Board with required particulars and documents.
* Inquiry by the Board: Upon receiving an application, the Board may conduct an inquiry to satisfy itself that all conditions are met.
* Decision: If satisfied, the Board will register the applicant as a Consent Manager and publish their particulars on its website. If not satisfied, the application is rejected with stated reasons.
4. Continuing Oversight and Board Powers
Registration brings the Consent Manager under the Board's direct regulatory control:
* Monitoring and Directions: If the Board finds a Consent Manager is not adhering to its obligations, it can direct the entity to take corrective measures.
* Suspension or Cancellation: If necessary in the interest of Data Principals, the Board has the power to suspend or cancel the registration of a Consent Manager after giving them an opportunity to be heard.
* Restrictions on Transfer: The control of a registered Consent Manager company (via sale, merger, etc.) cannot be transferred without the previous approval of the Board.
* Inquiry into Breaches: The Board is specifically empowered to inquire into breaches of any condition of registration and may impose penalties for such violations.
5. Implementation Timeline
The requirement for Consent Managers to be registered with the Board is subject to a specific commencement timeline:
* Notification Date: The initial notification was published on November 13, 2025.
* Enforcement Date: The provisions regarding the registration of Consent Managers (Section 6(9) and Rule 4) are scheduled to come into force one year after the notification.
* Effective Date: Consequently, the formal registration mechanism and the Board’s power to inquire into registration breaches will be active starting November 13, 2026

children.title: Fiduciary capacity to Data Principal
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, and the DPDP Rules, 2025, a Consent Manager is legally required to act in a fiduciary capacity in relation to the Data Principal,. This designation places a high standard of trust and legal obligation on the Consent Manager, ensuring they prioritize the interests of the individual over those of Data Fiduciaries.
1. Statutory Mandate of Accountability
The Act establishes the foundation for this relationship by stating that a Consent Manager must be accountable to the Data Principal and shall act on her behalf. This means that while a Consent Manager provides a technical platform for managing consent, its primary legal loyalty is to the individual, not the entities seeking to process the data,.
2. Defining the Fiduciary Capacity
The DPDP Rules, 2025, explicitly state that "the Consent Manager shall act in a fiduciary capacity in relation to the Data Principal",. In this context, the fiduciary duty includes:
* Conflict of Interest Prevention: The Consent Manager must strictly avoid conflicts of interest with Data Fiduciaries, including their promoters and key managerial personnel,.
* Structural Independence: They must implement measures to ensure that their directors, senior management, and key personnel do not have directorships, financial interests, employment, or beneficial ownership in any Data Fiduciary that could influence their actions,.
* Transparency: They must publish information about their own promoters and any person holding more than two percent of their shares to maintain transparency,.
3. Obligations in Service of the Data Principal
To fulfill its fiduciary role, the Consent Manager must provide tools and maintain records that empower the Data Principal:
* Record-Keeping: They must maintain a platform record of all consents given, denied, or withdrawn, as well as the notices that preceded those requests,.
* Accessibility: Upon request, they must provide the Data Principal with access to these records in a machine-readable form,.
* Integrity of Consent: They must ensure that the manner in which data is shared prevents the Consent Manager itself from being able to read the contents of the personal data,.
4. Board Oversight of the Fiduciary Relationship
The Data Protection Board of India acts as the regulator of this fiduciary relationship through the registration process:
* Registration Review: Before registering a Consent Manager, the Board must satisfy itself that the entity has the "financial condition and the general character of management" to fulfill these obligations,.
* Remedial Action: If a Consent Manager fails to adhere to its fiduciary duties or registration conditions, the Board has the power to issue directions, suspend registration, or cancel it entirely in the interest of Data Principals,.
5. Implementation Timeline
The provisions defining the accountability and fiduciary duties of Consent Managers (Section 6(8) and Rule 4) are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the initial notification, becoming formally enforceable in May 2027

children.title: Interoperable platform for consent lifecycle
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, and its accompanying 2025 Rules, the interoperable platform is the mandatory technical core of a Consent Manager's operations. It serves as the functional interface through which a Data Principal manages their entire consent lifecycle in a standardized and secure manner,.
1. Definition and Core Function
A Consent Manager is defined as a person registered with the Data Protection Board who acts as a single point of contact for the Data Principal. The Act mandates that this contact occurs through an accessible, transparent, and interoperable platform designed to enable four key actions:
* Giving consent for data processing,.
* Managing existing consents.
* Reviewing the status of consents and data sharing,.
* Withdrawing consent with the same ease it was given,.
2. Standards and Certification
To ensure the platform is truly "interoperable" across different Data Fiduciaries and services, the DPDP Rules, 2025, set strict registration conditions:
* Independent Certification: Before registration, the platform must be independently certified as consistent with data protection standards and the assurance framework published by the Board,.
* Technical Capacity: The applicant must demonstrate sufficient technical and operational capacity to maintain such a platform,.
* Primary Access: The Consent Manager must develop and maintain a website, app, or both as the primary means for the Data Principal to access these services,.
3. Managing the Consent Lifecycle
The platform is responsible for facilitating the flow of consent between the individual and various entities:
* Direct or Routed Consent: The platform must enable the Data Principal to give consent to an "onboarded" Data Fiduciary either directly or through another onboarded fiduciary who already maintains that individual's data (such as a bank or digital locker),.
* Comprehensive Record-Keeping: The platform must maintain a detailed record of the lifecycle, including:
   * Consents given, denied, or withdrawn,.
   * The specific notices that preceded or accompanied each consent request,.
   * The actual sharing of personal data with "transferee" Data Fiduciaries,.
* Machine-Readable Access: Upon request, the platform must provide the Data Principal with their consent records in a machine-readable form,.
* Retention: These lifecycle records must be maintained on the platform for at least seven years, unless a longer period is agreed upon or required by law,.
4. Technical Safeguards and "Data Blindness"
A critical obligation of the Consent Manager is to ensure the platform operates as a secure conduit without accessing the underlying data:
* Inability to Read Contents: The platform must be designed so that the manner of sharing personal data ensures the contents of the data are not readable by the Consent Manager itself,.
* Security Measures: The Consent Manager is required to take reasonable security safeguards to prevent any personal data breach within this platform,.
5. Enforcement and Implementation Timeline
The rollout of these platform requirements follows a staggered schedule:
* Registration Provisions: The sections and rules governing the registration of Consent Managers (including the certification of their platforms) are scheduled to come into force one year after the November 13, 2025, notification, meaning November 13, 2026,,.
* Substantive Obligations: The specific obligations of Consent Managers regarding the management of the consent lifecycle on the platform are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the notification, in May 2027

children.title: Maintain records of consent
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, and the DPDP Rules, 2025, a Consent Manager serves as a specialized, registered entity that acts as a single point of contact for a Data Principal to give, manage, review, and withdraw consent. A core pillar of this role is the mandatory requirement to maintain comprehensive records of the consent lifecycle to ensure transparency and accountability to the individual.
1. Scope of Record-Keeping (First Schedule, Part B)
A Consent Manager is legally obligated to maintain a detailed record on its platform regarding the following activities:
* Consent Status: Every instance of consent being given, denied, or withdrawn by the Data Principal.
* Associated Notices: The specific notices that either preceded or accompanied the requests for consent, ensuring there is a record of what information the individual was provided with before making a decision.
* Data Sharing Events: A record of the actual sharing of personal data with any "transferee" Data Fiduciary, providing a trail of where the individual's data has been sent.
2. Accessibility and Data Portability
To empower the Data Principal, the records maintained by the Consent Manager must be highly accessible:
* Direct Access: The platform must provide the Data Principal with direct access to these records at any time.
* Machine-Readable Form: Upon request, and in accordance with the terms of service, the Consent Manager must provide the information contained in these records in a machine-readable form. This ensures the individual can easily review or transfer their consent history.
3. Retention Period
The Act and Rules establish a long-term retention standard for these records:
* Seven-Year Minimum: Consent Managers must maintain these lifecycle records for a minimum period of seven years.
* Extensions: This period can be longer if the Data Principal and Consent Manager mutually agree to an extension or if such retention is required by another law for the time being in force.
4. Technical Integrity and "Data Blindness"
While the Consent Manager is required to keep records of the consent transactions, the framework includes a critical safeguard to protect the privacy of the underlying data:
* Non-Readable Contents: The Consent Manager must ensure that the technical manner of making personal data available or sharing it is designed so that the contents of the personal data are not readable by the Consent Manager itself. Essentially, they manage the "permission" to share data without having access to the "substance" of the data being shared.
5. Context of Fiduciary Duty
The duty to maintain these records is a key component of the Consent Manager's fiduciary capacity. Because they are accountable to the Data Principal and act on her behalf, these records serve as the primary evidence that the individual's choices regarding their personal data are being respected and correctly implemented across various Data Fiduciaries.
6. Enforcement Timeline
The substantive obligations for Consent Managers to maintain these records (Rule 4 and the First Schedule) are subject to a staggered commencement:
* Registration Start: The provisions allowing for the registration of Consent Managers come into force in November 13, 2026.
* Full Enforcement: The broader obligations, including the 7-year record-keeping mandate, are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the initial notification, making them legally enforceable in May 2027

children.title: Appeal within sixty days
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the requirement to file an appeal within sixty days is a critical procedural timeline for those seeking to challenge a decision made by the Data Protection Board of India. This mandate is part of the broader regulatory framework governing the Appellate Tribunal, which is designated as the Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT).
1. The Sixty-Day Filing Mandate
The Act specifies a strict window for legal recourse following a Board decision:
* Statutory Window: Every appeal against an order or direction made by the Board must be filed within a period of sixty days.
* Trigger Event: This sixty-day period begins from the date of receipt of the order or direction being appealed against.
* Exceptions for Delay: The Appellate Tribunal has the legal authority to entertain an appeal after the expiry of this sixty-day window if it is satisfied that the appellant had sufficient cause for the delay.
2. Digital Filing and Fee Requirements
In line with the Act’s "digital by design" philosophy, the process of filing within this sixty-day window must follow specific digital protocols:
* Digital Form: Appeals must be filed in a digital form as determined by the Appellate Tribunal.
* Digital Payment: The appeal must be accompanied by a fee, which must be paid digitally using the Unified Payments Interface (UPI) or another authorized payment system.
* Fee Amount: The fee is generally the same amount applicable to appeals under the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India Act, 1997, though the Chairperson of the Tribunal has the discretion to reduce or waive it.
3. Context within the Appellate Tribunal’s Function
The sixty-day filing rule is the first step in a process designed for speed and technical efficiency:
* Expedited Disposal: Once an appeal is presented, the Tribunal is required to deal with it as expeditiously as possible, with an endeavor to reach a final disposal within six months.
* Digital Office: The Tribunal operates as a digital office, meaning that from the receipt of the appeal within the sixty-day window to the final decision, proceedings may be conducted through techno-legal measures that do not require physical presence.
* Binding Outcomes: Orders passed following the appeal are executable as a decree of a civil court.
4. Enforcement and Implementation
While the Data Protection Board was established in November 2025, the legal provisions governing the right to appeal and the sixty-day timeline are subject to a staggered commencement.
* Effective Date: According to the enforcement notification, Section 29 (Appeal to Appellate Tribunal) and Rule 22 (Appellate Procedure) are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the initial November 13, 2025, notification.
* Practical Impact: This means the sixty-day window for appeals and the Tribunal’s jurisdiction over DPDP matters will be formally and legally active starting in May 2027

children.title: TDSAT as designated tribunal
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT) is formally designated as the Appellate Tribunal for matters arising under the Act. Rather than creating a new judicial body, the legislation leverages the existing framework of the TDSAT to provide a second tier of oversight for the decisions made by the Data Protection Board of India.
1. Statutory Designation and Legal Basis
The Act provides a clear definition in Section 2(a), stating that the "Appellate Tribunal" refers specifically to the TDSAT established under Section 14 of the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI) Act, 1997. To finalize this designation, Section 44(1) of the DPDP Act amends the TRAI Act to officially include "the Appellate Tribunal under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023" as one of the bodies for which the TDSAT performs its appellate functions.
2. Role within the Regulatory Framework
The Appellate Tribunal serves as the primary adjudicatory body for individuals or entities dissatisfied with the Board's findings:
* Appellate Authority: Any person aggrieved by an order or direction made by the Data Protection Board of India has the legal right to file an appeal before the TDSAT.
* Powers of Adjudication: Upon hearing an appeal, the Tribunal is authorized to pass orders that confirm, modify, or set aside the original decision made by the Board.
* Procedural Independence: While the Tribunal has the same powers as a civil court for executing its decrees, it is not strictly bound by the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. Instead, it is guided by the principles of natural justice and has the authority to regulate its own procedure.
3. Functioning as a Digital Office
In keeping with the Act's "digital by design" philosophy, the TDSAT is mandated to function as a digital office when hearing data protection cases.
* Online Mechanism: The entire lifecycle of an appeal—from receipt and filing to the final pronouncement of decisions—is intended to be conducted in an online or digital mode.
* Techno-Legal Measures: The Tribunal may adopt specific techno-legal measures to conduct proceedings without requiring the physical presence of the parties involved.
* Digital Filing and Fees: Appeals must be filed in a digital form and accompanied by a fee equivalent to those under the TRAI Act, which must be paid via digital systems like UPI.
4. Enforcement and Commencement Timelines
The legal designation of the TDSAT and its operational powers follow different implementation schedules:
* Designation (Section 44(1)): The amendment to the TRAI Act that officially designates the TDSAT as the Appellate Tribunal came into force on November 13, 2025, the date of the government's enforcement notification.
* Appellate Rights (Section 29): The substantive right for a person to actually file an appeal (and the corresponding procedural rules) is scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the notification.
* Full Implementation: Consequently, while the legal framework for the Tribunal was established in late 2025, it will only become fully functional for receiving and hearing data protection appeals in May 2027

children.title: Orders executable as civil court decrees
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, orders passed by the Appellate Tribunal are granted a specific legal status to ensure their effective enforcement: they are executable as a decree of a civil court. This provision ensures that the Tribunal's decisions carry the same legal weight and compulsory force as a judgment from the traditional judiciary.
1. Authority and Powers of Execution
To facilitate the enforcement of its decisions, the Act provides the Appellate Tribunal with the necessary legal machinery:
* Direct Execution: The Appellate Tribunal has the authority to execute its own orders directly, and for this specific purpose, it is vested with all the powers of a civil court.
* Transmission to Local Courts: Alternatively, the Tribunal may choose to transmit its order to a civil court having local jurisdiction. In such cases, the local civil court is required to execute the order as if it were a decree made by that court itself.
2. Context within the Regulatory Framework
The status of these orders as civil decrees is a cornerstone of the Act's specialized adjudicatory process:
* Designated Tribunal: The "Appellate Tribunal" refers to the Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT).
* Procedural Flexibility: While its orders are treated as civil decrees, the Tribunal itself is not bound by the strict procedure laid down by the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. Instead, it is guided by the principles of natural justice and has the power to regulate its own procedure.
* Digital Operations: The Tribunal is mandated to function as a digital office, utilizing "techno-legal measures" to conduct proceedings—such as receiving appeals and pronouncing decisions—without requiring the physical presence of the parties.
* Bar of Jurisdiction: To protect the integrity of this framework, no other civil court has the jurisdiction to entertain suits or proceedings regarding matters that the Board or the Appellate Tribunal are empowered to handle. No injunctions can be granted by any other court against actions taken under the Act.
3. Implementation Timeline
The legal mandate for the execution of these orders is subject to the Act's staggered commencement schedule:
* Wait Period: The provision establishing the executability of orders (Section 30) is scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the initial enforcement notification was published on November 13, 2025.
* Effective Date: Consequently, the formal legal power to execute the Tribunal's orders as civil court decrees will become active in May 2027

children.title: Based on nature and gravity of breach
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the "nature, gravity, and duration of the breach" is a primary statutory factor that the Data Protection Board of India must consider when determining the amount of a monetary penalty. This ensures that penalties are not arbitrary but are instead tailored to the specific circumstances of each violation.
1. Determination of Penalties (Section 33)
The Board is empowered to impose monetary penalties specified in the Schedule of the Act if it determines, following an inquiry, that a person has committed a significant breach of the Act or its rules.
2. Statutory Factors for Assessment
According to Section 33(2), while determining the specific amount of a penalty, the Board must have regard to the following matters:
* Nature, Gravity, and Duration: The Board evaluates how severe the breach was, the specific way it occurred, and how long the non-compliance lasted.
* Data Characteristics: The Board considers the type and nature of the personal data affected by the breach (e.g., whether it involved highly sensitive information).
* Recidivism: Whether the breach is of a repetitive nature.
* Financial Impact: Whether the person realized a financial gain or avoided a loss as a result of the breach.
* Mitigation Efforts: The timeliness and effectiveness of any actions taken by the person to mitigate the effects and consequences of the breach.
* Effectiveness and Proportionality: The Board ensures the penalty is proportionate while remaining effective enough to secure observance and deter future breaches.
* Impact on the Person: The likely overall impact of the penalty on the individual or entity.
3. Maximum Penalty Caps (The Schedule)
The "nature and gravity" of the breach are assessed against the maximum limits provided in the Act’s Schedule:
* Security Failures: For failing to take reasonable security safeguards to prevent a data breach, the penalty may extend to two hundred and fifty crore rupees.
* Children’s Data: For breaches of additional obligations in relation to children, the penalty can reach two hundred crore rupees.
* Notification Failures: For failing to notify the Board or affected Data Principals of a breach, the penalty may extend to two hundred crore rupees.
* General Breaches: For other breaches of the Act or its Rules, the penalty may extend to fifty crore rupees.
4. Procedural Requirements
The Board cannot impose these penalties without following specific legal procedures:
* Natural Justice: The person concerned must be given an opportunity of being heard before a penalty is finalized.
* Record of Reasons: The Board is required to record its reasons in writing for its actions and findings during and after an inquiry.
* Credit to Fund: All sums realized through these penalties are credited to the Consolidated Fund of India.
5. Enforcement Timeline
While the Data Protection Board was established in November 2025, the substantive powers to impose these penalties are subject to a staggered commencement. The sections governing penalties (Section 33) and the corresponding Schedule are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the initial notification, making them legally enforceable in May 2027

children.title: Consideration of gain or avoided loss
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the consideration of gain realized or loss avoided is a mandatory statutory factor that the Data Protection Board of India must evaluate when determining the appropriate amount for a monetary penalty. This requirement ensures that penalties are economically meaningful and prevent entities from profiting from non-compliance.
1. Definitions of Gain and Loss
The Act provides specific legal definitions to ensure clarity in how financial advantages or disadvantages are calculated:
* Gain: This includes a gain in property or the supply of services (whether temporary or permanent), as well as an opportunity to earn remuneration, greater remuneration, or any financial advantage beyond what is considered legitimate.
* Loss: This is defined as a loss in property or an interruption in the supply of services, or the loss of an opportunity to earn remuneration or gain a financial advantage through legitimate means.
2. Role in Penalty Determination
According to Section 33(2)(d), when the Board concludes an inquiry and determines that a significant breach has occurred, it must consider whether the person realized a gain or avoided a loss as a direct result of that breach. This factor is assessed alongside other critical elements, including:
* The nature, gravity, and duration of the breach.
* The type and nature of personal data affected.
* The repetitive nature of the breach.
* The timeliness and effectiveness of mitigation actions taken by the person.
3. Strategic Purpose: Deterrence and Proportionality
The inclusion of gain and avoided loss in the calculation is linked to the Board's broader mandate to ensure that penalties are proportionate and effective. By looking at the financial outcomes of a breach, the Board aims to:
* Secure Observance: Ensure that the cost of the penalty outweighs any potential financial benefit gained by ignoring the law.
* Deter Future Breaches: Discourage the person and others in the industry from viewing data breaches as a manageable "cost of doing business".
* Assess Likely Impact: Understand the overall economic effect the penalty will have on the specific individual or entity.
4. Impact on Penalty Scales
The assessment of financial gain or avoided loss occurs within a framework of high maximum penalties specified in The Schedule:
* For failing to take reasonable security safeguards (often the source of financial "avoided loss" through underinvestment in security), penalties can reach two hundred and fifty crore rupees.
* General breaches of the Act can result in penalties of up to fifty crore rupees.
5. Enforcement Timeline
The Board's authority to evaluate these financial factors and impose penalties under Section 33 is subject to a staggered commencement. According to the notification issued on November 13, 2025, these provisions are scheduled to come into force eighteen months later, meaning they will be legally active and enforceable in May 2027

children.title: Credited to Consolidated Fund of India
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the requirement that penalties be credited to the Consolidated Fund of India serves as the final step in the statutory enforcement process, ensuring that fines collected for data protection violations are treated as central government revenue.
1. The Statutory Mandate (Section 34)
The Act provides a clear and singular directive regarding the financial proceeds of enforcement actions. According to Section 34, "all sums realised by way of penalties imposed by the Board under this Act, shall be credited to the Consolidated Fund of India". This means that the Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) does not retain the funds it collects from fines; instead, they are deposited into the primary account of the Government of India.
2. Context of Monetary Penalties
These funds originate from the Board's power to impose penalties following an inquiry into a significant breach of the Act or its rules. The amounts directed to the Consolidated Fund can be substantial, based on the maximum caps set in The Schedule:
* Security Safeguards: Up to two hundred and fifty crore rupees for failing to prevent a data breach.
* Children’s Data & Notifications: Up to two hundred crore rupees for failing to protect minors or failing to notify the Board/individuals of a breach.
* Significant Data Fiduciary Obligations: Up to one hundred and fifty crore rupees.
3. Distinction from State "Public Funds"
The sources also reference the Consolidated Fund of India in a separate context involving the processing of personal data for subsidies and benefits. When the State provides services using "public funds," it includes expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India or the Consolidated Fund of a State. This highlights the Fund’s dual role in the Act: as the recipient of penalty revenue and as the source of funding for government programs that justify certain data processing activities.
4. Procedural Safeguards
Before any sum is realized and credited to the Fund, the Board must observe the principles of natural justice. This includes:
* Providing the concerned person an opportunity of being heard.
* Considering factors such as the nature, gravity, and duration of the breach.
* Determining if the person realized a financial gain or avoided a loss.
5. Enforcement Timeline
The provision for crediting penalties to the Consolidated Fund (Section 34) is not yet active. According to the enforcement notification issued on November 13, 2025, this section is subject to a staggered commencement.
* Implementation Date: Section 34 is scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the notification.
* Effective Date: Consequently, the requirement to credit penalties to the Consolidated Fund will be formally and legally enforceable starting in May 2027

children.title: Legal rights enforcement
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the processing of personal data for the purpose of enforcing any legal right or claim is granted a significant exemption from the majority of the Act's core obligations and rights framework. This ensures that the legal system and the assertion of rights are not hindered by strict data protection compliance requirements.
1. The Scope of the Exemption (Section 17)
According to Section 17(1)(a), the provisions of Chapter II (Obligations of Data Fiduciary), Chapter III (Rights and Duties of Data Principal), and Section 16 (Transfer of personal data outside India) shall not apply where the processing of personal data is necessary for enforcing any legal right or claim.
This means that when an entity is processing data to establish, exercise, or defend a legal position, it is generally exempt from:
* Notice and Consent Requirements: The need to provide notice or obtain affirmative consent before processing.
* Data Principal Rights: The individual's rights to access, correction, erasure, or grievance redressal regarding that specific data.
* Cross-Border Transfer Restrictions: The Central Government’s power to restrict the transfer of personal data to certain countries or territories.
2. Retained Obligations
While the exemption is broad, it is not absolute. Even when processing data to enforce legal rights, Data Fiduciaries must still comply with two critical "General Obligations" under Section 8:
* Ultimate Responsibility (Section 8(1)): The Data Fiduciary remains responsible for complying with the Act for any processing undertaken by it or on its behalf by a Data Processor.
* Security Safeguards (Section 8(5)): The fiduciary must protect the personal data in its possession by taking reasonable security safeguards to prevent personal data breaches.
3. The Larger Context of Exemptions
The "legal right" exemption sits within a category of "Special Provisions" designed for situations where the public interest or the functionality of the State takes precedence over individual data rights. This category includes processing necessary for:
* Judicial and Quasi-Judicial Functions: Processing by courts, tribunals, or regulatory bodies entrusted with judicial or supervisory functions.
* Prevention and Investigation of Offences: Processing in the interest of preventing, detecting, or investigating any offence or law contravention in India.
* Financial Defaults: Processing to ascertain the financial information, assets, and liabilities of a person who has defaulted on a loan from a financial institution.
* Corporate Restructuring: Processing for schemes of merger, amalgamation, or demerger approved by a competent authority.
4. Enforcement Timeline
According to the government notification issued on November 13, 2025, the provisions governing these exemptions are subject to a staggered commencement.
* Commencement Period: Section 17 is scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the notification.
* Effective Date: Consequently, the exemption for enforcing legal rights and claims will be formally and legally operational starting in May 2027

children.title: Judicial or quasi-judicial functions
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the processing of personal data for the performance of judicial or quasi-judicial functions is granted a broad exemption from many of the Act's substantive requirements. This ensures that courts, tribunals, and other legal bodies can discharge their duties without the procedural constraints typically imposed on Data Fiduciaries.
1. Scope of the Exemption (Section 17)
According to Section 17(1)(b), the Act’s primary obligations and rights frameworks do not apply to the processing of personal data by any court, tribunal, or other body in India entrusted by law with the performance of any judicial, quasi-judicial, regulatory, or supervisory function.
This exemption is conditional: the processing must be necessary for the performance of the specific function.
2. Extent of the Exemptions
When a body is performing a judicial or quasi-judicial function, it is exempt from the following:
* Chapter II (Obligations of Data Fiduciary): This includes requirements for notice, consent, and most general obligations.
* Chapter III (Rights and Duties of Data Principal): Individuals cannot exercise rights such as the right to access, correction, or erasure against data being processed for these functions.
* Section 16 (Transfer of personal data outside India): The restrictions on cross-border data transfers do not apply in this context.
3. Retained Obligations
Despite the broad nature of the exemption, judicial and quasi-judicial bodies are not exempt from two fundamental "General Obligations" under Section 8:
* Responsibility for Processing (Section 8(1)): The entity remains legally responsible for complying with the Act for any processing it undertakes or that is undertaken on its behalf.
* Security Safeguards (Section 8(5)): The entity must protect the personal data in its possession by taking reasonable security safeguards to prevent personal data breaches.
4. Context within the Regulatory Framework
The DPDP Act establishes two primary bodies that function in a quasi-judicial capacity:
* Data Protection Board of India (DPBI): In performing its functions, such as conducting inquiries, the Board is vested with the same powers as a civil court regarding summoning witnesses and receiving evidence.
* Appellate Tribunal (TDSAT): Designated as the Appellate Tribunal, it has the power to confirm, modify, or set aside Board orders and operates as a quasi-judicial body guided by the principles of natural justice.
The exemption ensures these bodies can process evidence and personal data during the course of an inquiry or appeal without needing the consent of the Data Principal involved.
5. Implementation Timeline
The provisions governing these exemptions are subject to the Act's staggered commencement schedule.
* Commencement: Section 17 is scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the initial notification issued on November 13, 2025.
* Effective Date: Consequently, the formal exemption for judicial and quasi-judicial functions will be legally active and operational starting in May 2027

children.title: Prevention and detection of offences
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the processing of personal data for the prevention and detection of offences is a primary ground for exemption from the Act's core obligations and individual rights. This framework ensures that law enforcement and regulatory activities are not obstructed by the standard requirements of notice, consent, and data principal access.
1. Core Exemption for Law Enforcement
According to Section 17(1)(c), the provisions of Chapter II (Data Fiduciary Obligations), Chapter III (Rights and Duties of Data Principals), and Section 16 (Cross-border transfers) shall not apply where personal data is processed in the interest of:
* Prevention, detection, investigation, or prosecution of any offence.
* Contravention of any law for the time being in force in India.
Under this exemption, entities (typically State agencies or fiduciaries acting under legal mandate) are not required to provide notice, obtain consent, or allow individuals to exercise rights like erasure or correction for data being used in a criminal context.
2. Sharing Data Between Fiduciaries
The Act provides a specific mechanism for the sharing of personal data between entities for crime-related purposes. Under Section 11(2), the restrictions that normally allow a Data Principal to see a summary of their shared data (under Section 11(1)(b) and (c)) do not apply if:
* The data is shared with another Data Fiduciary authorised by law.
* The sharing is pursuant to a request made in writing.
* The purpose is the prevention, detection, or investigation of offences or cyber incidents, or the prosecution and punishment of offences.
3. State Instrumentality Exemptions
The Central Government possesses the power under Section 17(2)(a) to notify specific instrumentalities of the State to be exempt from the entire Act (with the exception of security and accountability standards). This power can be exercised in the interest of:
* Maintenance of public order.
* Preventing incitement to any cognizable offence related to the sovereignty, integrity, or security of India.
4. Data Retention for Enforcement Purposes
While the Act generally requires the erasure of personal data once its purpose is served, Rule 8(3) of the DPDP Rules, 2025, establishes a mandatory retention period to assist the State.
* One-Year Retention: Data Fiduciaries must retain personal data, associated traffic data, and processing logs for a minimum of one year from the date of processing.
* Purpose: This retention is specifically for the purposes outlined in the Seventh Schedule, which includes the State's use of data for functions under any law or the disclosure of information to fulfill legal obligations (such as responding to law enforcement requests).
5. Retained Security Obligations
Even when the "prevention and detection of offences" exemption applies, a Data Fiduciary is not exempt from the general obligations found in Section 8(1) and 8(5). They must still:
* Remain Responsible: Be accountable for the processing of the data.
* Ensure Security: Take reasonable security safeguards to prevent a personal data breach.
6. Implementation Timeline
The provisions governing these exemptions and the related sharing/retention mandates are subject to the government’s staggered commencement schedule.
* Effective Date: Section 17 (Exemptions), Section 11 (Sharing), and Rule 8 (Retention) are all scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the November 13, 2025, notification.
* Enforcement: These law enforcement-related exemptions will be formally and legally operational in May 2027

children.title: Research, archiving, or statistical purposes
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, the processing of personal data for research, archiving, or statistical purposes is granted a conditional exemption from the Act's general provisions. This ensures that data-driven insights and historical preservation can continue without the full weight of standard regulatory obligations, provided specific safeguards are met.
1. The Core Exemption (Section 17)
According to Section 17(2)(b), the provisions of the Act shall not apply to the processing of personal data when it is necessary for these specific purposes. However, this exemption is strictly contingent on two factors:
* No Specific Decision-Making: The personal data must not be used to take any decision specific to a Data Principal.
* Adherence to Standards: The processing must be carried out in accordance with standards prescribed by the government.
2. Prescribed Standards for Processing (Second Schedule)
Rule 16 of the DPDP Rules, 2025, specifies that for this exemption to apply, the processing must follow the technical and organizational standards laid out in the Second Schedule. These standards include:
* Lawful Basis: The processing must still be carried out in a lawful manner.
* Data Minimization: Processing is limited only to the personal data necessary for achieving the research, archiving, or statistical purpose.
* Data Integrity: Fiduciaries must make reasonable efforts to ensure the completeness, accuracy, and consistency of the data.
* Storage Limitation: Data must only be retained for as long as it is required for the specific purpose or for compliance with any law.
* Security Safeguards: The entity must take reasonable security safeguards to prevent personal data breaches while the data is in its possession or control.
* Accountability: The person or entity determining the purpose and means of processing remains accountable for the effective observance of these standards.
3. Relationship with Other Exemptions
This exemption is part of a broader "Special Provisions" chapter. It is distinct from the Section 17(1) exemptions (such as for legal rights or judicial functions), which still require compliance with core security and accountability obligations. For research, archiving, and statistics, the entire Act is exempted, provided the Second Schedule standards are maintained.
4. Implementation Timeline
The provisions governing this exemption are subject to the government's staggered commencement schedule:
* Notification Date: November 13, 2025.
* Commencement Period: Both Section 17 and Rule 16 are scheduled to come into force eighteen months after the date of the notification.
* Effective Date: Consequently, the formal exemption for research, archiving, and statistical purposes will be legally operational starting in May 2027

children.title: Voluntary undertakings
children.content:
Under the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023, a voluntary undertaking serves as a specialized legal mechanism for self-correction and alternate dispute resolution. It allows a person or entity to commit to specific compliance actions to resolve proceedings before the Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) without necessarily facing immediate penalties.
1. Nature and Scope of Voluntary Undertakings
The Board has the authority to accept a voluntary undertaking from any person regarding their observance of the Act’s provisions.
* Timing: An undertaking can be proposed and accepted at any stage of a proceeding conducted by the Board.
* Content: The undertaking may include a commitment to take specific actions within a determined timeframe, refrain from certain actions, or even publicize the undertaking itself to ensure transparency.
* Variation: Once accepted, the terms of the undertaking can be varied by the Board, provided the person who gave the undertaking consents to the changes.
2. Legal Effect: A Bar on Proceedings
The most significant incentive for offering a voluntary undertaking is its legal finality.
* Cessation of Action: The acceptance of a voluntary undertaking by the Board constitutes a bar on further proceedings under the Act regarding the specific contents and issues covered by that undertaking.
* Exception for Non-Compliance: This bar is only lifted if the person fails to adhere to the agreed-upon terms.
3. Consequences of Breach
